words.shortDefinition: Independent components that don't affect each other when changed.
words.simpleDefinition: Change one, don't break another — auth and permissions independent. Math term (perpendicular) borrowed for modularity. Goal of separation of concerns.
words.longDefinition: In software architecture, refers to system components or design decisions that can be changed independently without impacting other parts. Borrowed from mathematics where orthogonal vectors are perpendicular.
words.connector: Think of orthogonal like Lego blocks — swap red for blue (change component) without rebuilding entire structure.
words.techContext: Used when discussing modular design, separation of concerns, and clean architecture.
words.exampleSentence: Our authentication and authorization systems are orthogonal, so we can swap auth providers without touching permissions.
words.etymology: from Greek orthos ("straight") + gōnia ("angle") — literally "right-angled."

words.shortDefinition: The degree of interdependence between software modules.
words.simpleDefinition: How tangled modules are — low coupling = independent, high coupling = fragile. Change payment module, breaks order module = high coupling.
words.longDefinition: Measures how closely connected different modules or components are. Low coupling means modules are independent and changes in one module minimally affect others. High coupling creates fragile systems where changes ripple across boundaries.
words.connector: Think of coupling like train cars — tightly coupled (welded) = move one, move all. Loosely coupled (hooks) = disconnect easily.
words.techContext: Fundamental metric in architecture reviews and refactoring discussions. Usually contrasted with cohesion.
words.exampleSentence: We reduced coupling by introducing interfaces so the payment module doesn't depend directly on Stripe's SDK.
words.etymology: from Old French cople ("bond, link") via Latin copula ("bond, tie").

words.shortDefinition: The degree to which elements within a module belong together.
words.simpleDefinition: How focused a module is — high cohesion = does one thing well. Low cohesion = UserService handles auth + email + PDF = split it.
words.longDefinition: Measures how focused and related the responsibilities within a single module are. High cohesion means a module does one thing well. Low cohesion indicates a module is doing unrelated tasks and should likely be split.
words.connector: Think of cohesion like restaurant menu — high cohesion = Italian place (pasta, pizza), low cohesion = serves sushi, tacos, BBQ.
words.techContext: Key indicator of module quality. High cohesion with low coupling is the architectural ideal.
words.exampleSentence: The UserService had low cohesion because it handled both authentication and email notifications, so we split it.
words.etymology: from Latin cohaesus, past participle of cohaerere ("to stick together").

words.shortDefinition: A pattern that encapsulates data access logic behind a collection-like interface.
words.simpleDefinition: Collection-like interface for data — business logic calls .findById(), doesn't care if Postgres or Redis. Centralizes data access.
words.longDefinition: Mediates between the domain layer and data mapping layers using a collection-like interface for accessing domain objects. The repository pattern centralizes data access logic and provides a cleaner separation between business logic and persistence.
words.connector: Think of repository like library checkout desk — ask for book by title (findById), desk handles where it's stored (DB details).
words.techContext: Common in domain-driven design and layered architectures. Often discussed alongside the Unit of Work pattern.
words.exampleSentence: We use the repository pattern so our business logic doesn't care whether data comes from Postgres or Redis.
words.etymology: from Latin repositorium ("storehouse") from reponere ("to put back, store").

words.shortDefinition: An object model of the domain that incorporates both behavior and data.
words.simpleDefinition: Objects with data AND business logic — Order knows canBeCanceled(). Contrasts with anemic (data-only) models. Core of DDD.
words.longDefinition: A representation of meaningful real-world concepts in code where domain objects contain both data and business logic. Contrasts with anemic domain models where objects are just data containers.
words.connector: Think of domain model like real-world rules in code — Order object enforces 'can't cancel after shipping' just like store policy.
words.techContext: Central to domain-driven design. Differentiates object-oriented design from procedural or transaction script approaches.
words.exampleSentence: Our domain model handles all business rules, so the Order object knows when it can be canceled or refunded.
words.etymology: domain from Latin dominium ("property, ownership"); model from Latin modulus ("small measure").

words.shortDefinition: A layer that defines an application's boundary and coordinates application activity.
words.simpleDefinition: Orchestrates workflows — calls repo, sends email, logs event. Sits between presentation and domain. Coordinates, doesn't contain business logic.
words.longDefinition: Establishes a set of available operations and coordinates the application's response in each operation. The service layer encapsulates the business logic and provides a well-defined API for clients while delegating domain logic to domain objects.
words.connector: Think of service layer like orchestra conductor — coordinates musicians (repo, email, logger), doesn't play instruments itself.
words.techContext: Common in layered architectures. Sits between presentation and domain layers to orchestrate workflows.
words.exampleSentence: The service layer coordinates the transaction: it calls the repository, sends the email, and logs the event.
words.etymology: service from Latin servitium ("slavery, servitude"); layer from Old English leger ("bed, stratum").

words.shortDefinition: A layer that moves data between domain objects and a database while keeping them independent.
words.simpleDefinition: Translates DB rows ↔ domain objects — UserMapper converts row to User. Domain objects ignorant of DB schema. Contrasts with Active Record.
words.longDefinition: Separates the in-memory domain objects from the database schema. The mapper handles all loading and storing of objects, allowing domain objects to remain ignorant of the database structure.
words.connector: Think of data mapper like translator — speaks database (rows/columns) and domain (objects), keeps both sides independent.
words.techContext: Contrasts with Active Record pattern. Provides better separation of concerns but requires more boilerplate code.
words.exampleSentence: We use the data mapper pattern so our domain models don't inherit from any ORM base class.
words.etymology: data from Latin datum ("something given"); mapper from Latin mappa ("napkin, cloth").

words.shortDefinition: A pattern where domain objects carry both data and database access methods.
words.simpleDefinition: Object = DB row + save/delete methods — user.save() persists directly. Rails pattern. Simpler than Data Mapper, but couples to DB.
words.longDefinition: An object that wraps a row in a database table, encapsulates database access, and adds domain logic on that data. Each instance corresponds to a single row and knows how to save, update, and delete itself.
words.connector: Think of Active Record like self-checkout — item knows how to scan itself. Data Mapper = cashier scans for you.
words.techContext: Popular in Rails and other frameworks. Simpler than Data Mapper but couples domain objects to database structure.
words.exampleSentence: Rails uses Active Record, so calling user.save() persists the object directly to the database.
words.etymology: active from Latin activus ("practical, operative"); record from Latin recordari ("to remember").

words.shortDefinition: A pattern that maintains a list of objects affected by a transaction and coordinates changes.
words.simpleDefinition: Batches changes, writes once — track new order, updated user, deleted address. Call commit(), all succeed or all fail. Transactional consistency.
words.longDefinition: Tracks all changes made during a business transaction and writes them out as a single unit. Handles ordering of database operations, manages conflicts, and ensures that all changes succeed or fail together.
words.connector: Think of unit of work like shopping cart — add items (track changes), checkout (commit) sends all at once, not one-by-one.
words.techContext: Essential for transactional consistency. Common in ORMs like Entity Framework and SQLAlchemy.
words.exampleSentence: The unit of work batches all our changes so we only hit the database once at commit time.
words.etymology: unit from Latin unitas ("oneness"); work from Old English weorc ("deed, action").

words.shortDefinition: A pattern that defers loading an object's data until it's actually needed.
words.simpleDefinition: Load when accessed, not upfront — user.getOrders() fetches on demand. Reduces initial load, but beware N+1 queries.
words.longDefinition: An object that doesn't contain all of the data you need but knows how to get it when required. Reduces initial load time and memory footprint by fetching related data only when accessed.
words.connector: Think of lazy load like opening apps on phone — don't load all into RAM at boot, launch when tapped (accessed).
words.techContext: Common in ORMs for loading related entities. Can cause N+1 query problems if not managed carefully.
words.exampleSentence: We lazy load the user's order history because most requests don't need it.
words.etymology: lazy from Middle Low German lasich ("languid, weak"); load from Old English lad ("way, course").

words.shortDefinition: Data Transfer Object - a simple object that carries data between processes.
words.simpleDefinition: Data container for transfer — API returns UserDTO, internal model changes don't break clients. No business logic, just fields.
words.longDefinition: An object that carries data between processes to reduce the number of method calls. DTOs are simple data containers with no business logic, often used to aggregate data for transfer across network or process boundaries.
words.connector: Think of DTO like shipping box — carries stuff (data) between places (processes), empty container with no logic.
words.techContext: Common in distributed systems and API design. Decouples external contracts from internal domain models.
words.exampleSentence: We return DTOs from our API so internal model changes don't break client contracts.
words.etymology: acronym for Data Transfer Object, popularized by Martin Fowler in Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (2002).

words.shortDefinition: An object that encapsulates access to an external system or resource.
words.simpleDefinition: Wraps external API — PaymentGateway hides Stripe vs PayPal differences. Easier interface, testable via mocks.
words.longDefinition: Wraps the API of an external system with an interface that's easier to use or more suitable for your application. Isolates the complexity of external communication and provides a point for testing and substitution.
words.connector: Think of gateway like universal adapter — plug your device (app) into any outlet (Stripe, PayPal), adapter handles differences.
words.techContext: Used to wrap third-party APIs, legacy systems, or external services. Enables easier testing via mocking.
words.exampleSentence: We built a payment gateway to abstract away the differences between Stripe and PayPal.
words.etymology: from gate (Old English geat) + way (Old English weg) — literally "a way through a gate."

words.shortDefinition: An immutable object whose equality is based on value, not identity.
words.simpleDefinition: Immutable, value-based equality — two Money(5, 'USD') are equal. No identity. Email, Money, DateRange common examples. DDD concept.
words.longDefinition: A small object that represents a descriptive aspect of the domain with no conceptual identity. Two value objects are equal if their values are equal. Always immutable to avoid aliasing bugs.
words.connector: Think of value object like $5 bill — two $5 bills are equal (value), don't care which specific bill (identity).
words.techContext: Core concept in domain-driven design. Common examples include Money, DateRange, Address, or EmailAddress.
words.exampleSentence: We made Email a value object so two instances with the same address are considered equal.
words.etymology: value from Latin valere ("to be strong, be worth"); object from Latin obiectus ("thing thrown before").

words.shortDefinition: A boundary within which a particular domain model is defined and applicable.
words.simpleDefinition: Model boundary — 'User' in billing = paying customer, 'User' in support = includes trials. Same term, different meaning per context. DDD core.
words.longDefinition: A central pattern in domain-driven design that defines the limits of a model's applicability. Within a bounded context, terms have specific meanings and the model is internally consistent. Different contexts may use the same terms differently.
words.connector: Think of bounded context like country borders — 'football' means soccer in UK (context), American football in US (context).
words.techContext: Critical for microservices architecture and team organization. Helps manage complexity in large systems.
words.exampleSentence: In the billing context, 'user' means a paying customer, but in the support context, it includes free trial accounts.
words.etymology: bounded from Old French bonde ("limit"); context from Latin contextus ("a joining together").

codeExample.code:
# Billing Context:
User:
  - subscriptionId
  - paymentMethod

# Support Context:
User:
  - ticketHistory
  - supportTier

words.shortDefinition: A cluster of domain objects treated as a single unit for data changes.
words.simpleDefinition: Cluster with one root — Order (root) contains OrderItems + ShippingAddress. External code only touches Order. Transactional boundary.
words.longDefinition: A pattern in domain-driven design where related objects are bound together with a root entity acting as the entry point. External objects can only reference the aggregate root, ensuring consistency and enforcing invariants.
words.connector: Think of aggregate like car (Order) — has parts (items, address), but you control car (root), not individual tires (children).
words.techContext: Defines transactional boundaries in domain models. Each aggregate has exactly one root entity.
words.exampleSentence: The Order aggregate includes OrderItems and ShippingAddress, but external code only touches Order directly.
words.etymology: from Latin aggregatus, past participle of aggregare ("to add to a flock") from ad- + grex ("flock").

words.shortDefinition: A domain model where objects contain data but little or no behavior.
words.simpleDefinition: Just data, no behavior — Order has fields but OrderService has logic. Anti-pattern. Separates data from behavior, violates OOP.
words.longDefinition: An anti-pattern where domain objects are just data containers with getters and setters, while all business logic lives in service classes. Contradicts object-oriented principles by separating data from behavior.
words.connector: Think of anemic model like mannequin — looks like person (has data), but can't move (no behavior).
words.techContext: Considered an anti-pattern by domain-driven design advocates, though sometimes pragmatic for simple CRUD applications.
words.exampleSentence: Our domain model was anemic—just POJOs with getters—so all the business rules ended up scattered across service classes.
words.etymology: anemic from Greek anaimia ("lack of blood") from an- ("without") + haima ("blood"); model from Latin modulus.

words.shortDefinition: An architecture organized into horizontal layers where each layer depends only on layers below it.
words.simpleDefinition: Horizontal stack — presentation calls service, service calls repository. Each layer depends only on layer below. Classic enterprise pattern.
words.longDefinition: Divides software into layers such as presentation, business logic, and data access. Each layer has a specific role and depends only on the layer directly beneath it, promoting separation of concerns and modular design.
words.connector: Think of layered architecture like building floors — penthouse (UI) sits on offices (services) sits on foundation (data), can't skip floors.
words.techContext: Classic enterprise architecture pattern. Often implemented as MVC, three-tier, or n-tier architectures.
words.exampleSentence: We use a layered architecture: the API layer calls the service layer, which calls repositories for data access.
words.etymology: layer from Old English leger ("bed, stratum"); architecture from Greek arkhitekton ("master builder").

codeExample.code:
# Typical layers:
presentation/    # UI, controllers
application/     # use cases, orchestration
domain/          # business logic
infrastructure/  # DB, external services

words.shortDefinition: An architecture that isolates core logic from external concerns via ports and adapters.
words.simpleDefinition: Ports and Adapters — core logic center, ports (interfaces), adapters (implementations). Swap DB/UI without touching core. DDD favorite.
words.longDefinition: Also called Ports and Adapters. Places business logic at the center, with ports defining interfaces and adapters implementing them for external systems. Allows the core to be independent of frameworks, databases, and UI.
words.connector: Think of hexagonal like plug socket (port) — same socket (interface), swap phone charger for laptop charger (adapter).
words.techContext: Popular in domain-driven design. Makes testing easier by allowing mock adapters for external dependencies.
words.exampleSentence: Hexagonal architecture let us run the same core logic with both a web UI and a CLI by swapping adapters.
words.etymology: hexagonal from Greek hex ("six") + gōnia ("angle"); coined by Alistair Cockburn in 2005.

words.shortDefinition: A design principle where each part of a system addresses a separate concern.
words.simpleDefinition: Each module handles one thing — validation in validators, data access in repos, HTTP in controllers. Minimal overlap.
words.longDefinition: The process of breaking a program into distinct features that overlap in functionality as little as possible. Each module or layer handles one aspect of the functionality, reducing coupling and improving maintainability.
words.connector: Think of separation of concerns like kitchen crew — chef cooks, server delivers, cashier handles payment. Don't mix roles.
words.techContext: Foundational principle behind layering, modularity, and single responsibility. Enables independent development and testing.
words.exampleSentence: We applied separation of concerns by moving all validation logic out of controllers into dedicated validator classes.
words.etymology: coined by Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1974; concern from Latin concernere ("to relate to").

words.shortDefinition: A technique where objects receive their dependencies from external sources rather than creating them.
words.simpleDefinition: Pass dependencies in, don't create them — constructor(repo: UserRepository) injected, not new UserRepository() inside. Enables mocking.
words.longDefinition: A form of inversion of control where dependencies are provided to a component rather than the component creating or finding them. Enables loose coupling, easier testing, and better separation of concerns.
words.connector: Think of DI like catering — ingredients (dependencies) delivered, don't grow your own vegetables (new Database()).
words.techContext: Core pattern in Spring, Angular, and modern frameworks. Enables constructor injection, setter injection, or interface injection.
words.exampleSentence: We use dependency injection so our tests can pass in a mock database instead of hitting the real one.
words.etymology: dependency from Latin dependere ("to hang from"); injection from Latin iniectio ("a throwing in").

words.shortDefinition: A principle where framework code calls application code, not the other way around.
words.simpleDefinition: Framework calls you, not vice versa — React decides when to render component. DI is a form of IoC. Hollywood Principle.
words.longDefinition: A design principle where the flow of control is inverted compared to traditional programming. Instead of your code calling libraries, the framework calls your code at specific points. Dependency injection is a specific form of IoC.
words.connector: Think of IoC like answering phone — you wait (define callback), phone system calls you (framework invokes), not you calling phone.
words.techContext: Fundamental to modern frameworks like Spring, React, and Express. Often implemented via callbacks, events, or dependency injection.
words.exampleSentence: React uses inversion of control—you define components and React decides when to render them.
words.etymology: inversion from Latin inversio ("a turning upside down"); control from Medieval Latin contrarotulum ("register").

words.shortDefinition: A simplified interface that makes a complex subsystem easier to use.
words.simpleDefinition: Simple interface over complex subsystem — AWSFacade.processFile() hides S3, SQS, Lambda details. Reduces client coupling.
words.longDefinition: Provides a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. Defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem easier to use by hiding its complexity and dependencies.
words.connector: Think of facade like hotel concierge — one call arranges taxi, dinner, tickets. Don't call each service directly.
words.techContext: Common when wrapping legacy code or complex libraries. Reduces coupling between subsystems and client code.
words.exampleSentence: We built a facade over the AWS SDK so our application code doesn't need to know about all the S3, SQS, and Lambda details.
words.etymology: from French façade ("front of a building") from Italian facciata, from faccia ("face").

words.shortDefinition: A pattern that converts one interface into another that clients expect.
words.simpleDefinition: Translator between interfaces — LoggerAdapter wraps OldLogger to match new Logger interface. Enables reuse, integrates legacy code.
words.longDefinition: Allows classes with incompatible interfaces to work together by wrapping one interface to match another. Acts as a translator between two different interfaces, enabling reuse of existing code.
words.connector: Think of adapter like travel plug — European device (old interface) plugs into US outlet (new interface) via adapter.
words.techContext: Common when integrating third-party libraries or legacy code. Also called a wrapper pattern.
words.exampleSentence: We wrote an adapter to make the old authentication system work with our new user service interface.
words.etymology: from Latin adaptare ("to fit, adjust") from ad- ("to") + aptare ("to fit").

words.shortDefinition: A pattern that defines a family of algorithms and makes them interchangeable.
words.simpleDefinition: Encapsulate algorithms — DomesticShipping vs InternationalShipping implement same interface. Swap at runtime. Replaces conditionals with polymorphism.
words.longDefinition: Encapsulates algorithms or business rules into separate classes with a common interface, allowing them to vary independently from clients that use them. Clients can choose which strategy to use at runtime.
words.connector: Think of strategy like payment methods — credit card, PayPal, Apple Pay all implement 'pay', client chooses which.
words.techContext: Useful for replacing conditional logic with polymorphism. Common in payment processing, sorting, or validation scenarios.
words.exampleSentence: We use the strategy pattern for shipping calculations—domestic, international, and express are different strategy implementations.
words.etymology: from Greek strategia ("generalship, office of a general") from strategos ("general").

words.shortDefinition: A pattern where objects subscribe to be notified of changes in another object.
words.simpleDefinition: Subscribe to changes — order ships, notify email/SMS/webhook observers. One-to-many. Foundation of pub/sub, reactive programming.
words.longDefinition: Defines a one-to-many dependency where when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified automatically. Promotes loose coupling by allowing subjects and observers to vary independently.
words.connector: Think of observer like newsletter subscribers — publisher sends update (event), all subscribers notified automatically.
words.techContext: Foundation of event-driven architectures and reactive programming. Implemented in pub/sub systems, event emitters, and reactive streams.
words.exampleSentence: Our notification system uses the observer pattern—when an order ships, multiple observers handle email, SMS, and webhook notifications.
words.etymology: from Latin observare ("to watch, note, heed") from ob- ("before") + servare ("to keep, watch").
